coredata.dc:description: Metal corrosion has caused serious waste of resources and materials, and also caused great pollution to the environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a green, fast, and simple method for corrosion sensing. Herein, a phytic acid carbon dots (PA-CDs) based green “off–on” fluorescence sensor was developed for OH− and H+ detection in metal corrosion. The fluorescence of PA-CDs gradually turned off as the concentration of OH− increased from 20 to 340 μmol/L, while its fluorescence was turned on with the addition of 60 ∼ 230 μmol/L of H+ under ultrasonic treatment. This can be utilized for the detection of OH− and H+, with estimated limits of detection at 7.33 μmol/L and 20.34 μmol/L, respectively. The “off-on” behavior of PA-CDs towards the corrosion species OH− and H+ can be attributed to its enhanced aggregation and disaggregation, which are induced by the deprotonation and protonation of the −H2PO4 functional groups. The PA-CDs were loaded onto the film and subsequently employed for Q235 steel packaging. The results revealed that the as-proposed green fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon can be visually observed after the onset of corrosion on the steel sheet, thereby demonstrating the potential application of PA-CDs film for early-stage corrosion monitoring.

head.ce:title: “Off-on” fluorescence sensor based on phytic acid carbon dots for OH−/H+ detection in metal corrosion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal corrosion has caused serious waste of resources and materials, and also caused great pollution to the environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a green, fast, and simple method for corrosion sensing. Herein, a phytic acid carbon dots (PA-CDs) based green “off–on” fluorescence sensor was developed for OH− and H+ detection in metal corrosion. The fluorescence of PA-CDs gradually turned off as the concentration of OH− increased from 20 to 340 μmol/L, while its fluorescence was turned on with the addition of 60 ∼ 230 μmol/L of H+ under ultrasonic treatment. This can be utilized for the detection of OH− and H+, with estimated limits of detection at 7.33 μmol/L and 20.34 μmol/L, respectively. The “off-on” behavior of PA-CDs towards the corrosion species OH− and H+ can be attributed to its enhanced aggregation and disaggregation, which are induced by the deprotonation and protonation of the −H2PO4 functional groups. The PA-CDs were loaded onto the film and subsequently employed for Q235 steel packaging. The results revealed that the as-proposed green fluorescence “turn-off” phenomenon can be visually observed after the onset of corrosion on the steel sheet, thereby demonstrating the potential application of PA-CDs film for early-stage corrosion monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion is a global issue that poses severe threats to structural security, life safety, environment, and economy. According to studies by Koch et [1] and Hou et al. [2], corrosion costs amounted to approximately 2.5 trillion dollars in the world in the year 2015, and 2.1 trillion RMB in China. Unfortunately, the cost of corrosion remains continuously increasing. Once corrosion is visible to the naked eye, it has already become a severe issue. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a fast, in-situ, and non-destructive method for the detection of metal corrosion at its early stage. This enables timely maintenance interventions to prevent severe corrosion and degradation of metal materials [3,4]. At present, the common technologies for metal corrosion detection include the ultrasonic method [5], X-ray detection method [6], infrared image technology [7], electromagnetic method [8], electrochemical method [9], eddy current method [10], and so on. However, some of these detection methods are costly, time-consuming and laborious, and require external energy [11]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop green, fast, facile, and non-destructive warning sensors for the identification of corrosion in the early stage.

Corrosion is an electrochemical process. The anodic reactions include the oxidation of the metal ions. This process involves the formation of metal ions and hydrogen ions, as shown below [12]:
M → Mn++ne−
Mn++nH2O → M(OH)n + nH+

At the same time, a reduction reaction of dissolved oxygen occurs at the cathode [13,14], this process involves the formation of hydroxyl ions.
O2 + H2O + 4e−→4OH−

According to the aforementioned electrochemical process, metal corrosion involves the generation of H+ and OH−, which are not only the generated corrosion products but also the corrosion species in the environment [12]. Therefore, it is of great significance to recognize OH− and H+ to identify the corrosion environment and indicate the corrosion in the early stage. Due to the high accuracy and all-range pH response, pH meters are widely applied for pH sensing [15], but they can only be used in solutions, which limits their application in the corrosion detection field. As the typical pH indicator, phenolphthalein has been added into the anti-corrosion coating in order to indicate the occurrence of corrosion, but it can only be applied to an alkaline environment [14], and the resulting pink color has been reported to disappear over time [15]. For corrosion monitoring purposes, several novel pH indicators have been prepared [16]. For example, Augustyniak and Ming prepared a novel compound named spiro [1H-isoindole-1,9′ −[9H] xanthen]-3(2H)-1,3,6-bis(diethylamino)-2-[(1-methylethy lidene) amino] (“FD1”), as a “turn-on” early aluminum corrosion detector at acid pH [13]. The non-fluorescence FD1 showed yellow fluorescence at low pH due to the protonation. However, the design and preparation of FD1 are complex, and can only be applied in the acid environment. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a green, simple, and sensitive fluorescence indicator capable of discerning both OH− and H+ concentrations induced by corrosion.

Carbon dot (CDs) is the most extensively investigated member of the nano-carbon family, exhibiting exceptional fluorescence properties that have propelled its potential applications in optoelectronics, bio-imaging, sensing, and other related fields [17–19]. The fluorescence of CDs is significantly influenced by the distribution state, whether it is homogeneously dispersed or aggregated [20,21]. This aspect plays a crucial role in enhancing the efficiency of fluorescent-based pH sensors for the monitoring of OH− and H+ in corrosion areas. For the construction of aggregation-disaggregation-based pH sensor, Nayak et al. [22] reported that the fluorescent CDs were found to be in the aggregated state at neutral pH and exhibited yellow fluorescence attributed to the formation of amide-conjugated networks. The CDs underwent a transition to homogeneous dispersion and exhibited blue fluorescence in the alkaline condition attributed to the hydrolysis of amide bonds. However, the amide-conjugated networks can only be formed under high temperature and long-term hydrothermal treatment.

In addition to the formation of amide-conjugated networks, altering the zeta potential through protonation and deprotonation processes is a facile approach for constructing aggregation-disaggregation-based pH sensors. However, at present studies, the protonation and deprotonation processes mainly depend on the carboxyl, hydroxy, and amino groups which can only provide one active site. In comparison, the phosphoric group possesses two protons, thereby offering more active sites for protonation and deprotonation processes [23]. This characteristic facilitates the induction of CDs aggregation or disaggregation in response to pH variations. Phatic acid (PA, C6H18O24P6) contains six phosphoric groups, and also a good chelating agent [24], has been served as a precursor for the preparation CDs. The CDs derived from PA contain rich phosphoric groups. Therefore, PA serves as an ideal precursor in the preparation of CDs for the construction of aggregation-disaggregation-based OH− and H+ ions sensor. However, the reported CDs derived from PA need to employ other carbon-containing compounds as the carbon source. The study on the preparation of CDs utilizing PA as both a carbon source and phosphorus source for H+/OH− detection has not been reported yet.

In the present study, PA was served as a precursor for the synthesize of PA-CDs via facile hydrothermal treatment. The PA-CDs were rich in phosphoric groups, and prepared for the fabrication of aggregation-disaggregation-based OH− and H+ sensor for corrosion monitoring. The formation, optical properties, and characteristics of PA-CDs were comprehensively discussed. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–vis spectra, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were carried out to elucidate the underlying “off–on” fluorescence response mechanism. PA-CDs were further fixed on PVA/starch film to prepare the portable test film for the detection of metal corrosion, and its fluorescence response to OH− was investigated under 365 nm UV light. The production cost and benefits of PA-CDs-based test films were analyzed to evaluate its economic feasibility. This study provides a green, fast, facile, and sensitive strategy for early-stage corrosion sensing.

PA solution (mass fraction: 70 %), which was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China), was employed as the precursor in the preparation of PA-CDs. A stock solution of OH− and H+ in 0.01 mol/L was prepared by diluting an appropriate amount of NaOH and HCl (Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin, China) into 0.10 mol/L, and then progressively diluted to 0.01 mol/L. PVA/starch film, which was obtained from Guangxi State Farms Mingyang Starch Development Co. Ltd., was used in the preparation of PA-CDs indicator film. Ammonia solution (25 %, Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., LTD, China) was used to examine the response property of PA-CDs film to OH−. Q235 carbon steel sheets with dimensions of 50 × 25 × 2 mm acted as the corrosion material to examine the corrosion warning property of PA-CDs indicator film. All the reagents used in the experiments are of analytical grade purity, and no further purifications were performed before their use. Ultra-pure water (18.2 kMΩ) produced by a Millipore-Q water system was used during the synthesis and preparation of all the solutions.

PA-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of PA. In a typical procedure, 2 mL of PA was dissolved into 18 mL of ultra-pure water and stirred to form a homogeneous solution. The obtained PA solution was transferred into a 50 mL PPL-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at the condition of 140 ∼ 260 °C for 4 ∼ 12 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature naturally, then filtered through 0.22 μm membrane via millipore syringe filters to remove the large particle impurity, and dialyzed against water in a dialysis tube (1000 Da) to remove the remaining salts and tiny fragments. The obtained PA-CDs show a transparent light brown color and emit yellow-green fluorescence under a UV lamp.

The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of PA-CDs were recorded by an LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) in the scan range of 300 ∼ 550 nm. A 150 W Xenon lamp was used as the excitation light source and the scanning range was 200 ∼ 800 nm at normal temperature. Raman spectra of PA-CDs under different hydrothermal temperature were recorded by the LabRAM HR Evolution Laser confocal Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Scientific, Japan) with a 350 nm laser and 1 cm−1 resolution. UV–Vis spectra were measured on a UT-1901 UV − Vis spectroscopy (Pekin Persee, China) at normal temperature in the scan range of 200 ∼ 800 nm. A dual beam ZF-1 benchtop UV analyzer (Lichen, China) was employed to observe the fluorescence of PA-CDs at normal temperature in a dark house. A Talos F200S G2 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) was employed to examine the enlarged areas of the samples at 200 kV acceleration voltage. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo ScientificTM ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer equipped (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray as the excitation source (1486.6 eV) operating at 100 W. The excitation source for the XPS test with a beam spot of 400 μ m. The vacuum in the analysis chamber was better than 2.0 × 10−7 mbar, the operating voltage was 12 kV, and the filament current was 6 mA. With a flux energy of 50 eV and a step size of 0.1 eV, the narrow spectrum scan has a number of scans exceeding five. Avantage software was used to analyze and fit the measurements, and charge correction was performed at the binding energy of C 1 s = 284.8 eV. All spectra were fitted using the Gauss-Lorentz product (GLP) function and the Smart background. During the fitting process, the range of the full width at half maxima (FWHM) was 0.5–2 eV, and the L/G Mix (%) index was 30 %. The fitted curves were optimized based on minimization of ∑χ2* values and the Abbe Criterion. Crystallinity of the PA-CDs samples was analyzed by a Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffraction meter (XRD, Rigaku, Japan). Measure were performed at Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) and Ni sheet filtering in the 2θ range of 5°∼80°, with a rate of 10°/min, an accelerating voltage of 150 kV, and a current of 40 mA. The change of the surface functionalization of the synthesized PA-CDs before and after the addition of OH− were investigated via the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets in the wavenumber range of 400 ∼ 4000 cm−1 and each spectrum represented an average of 32 consecutive scans at 2 cm−1 resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PA-CDs to OH−
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence response of PA-CDs to OH−, 1.00 mL of 50 μg/mL PA-CDs were first dropped into a series of tubes. Next, 0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 120, 140, 160, 200, 240, 280, 320, and 360 μL of 0.01 mol/L OH− were added to each tube, mixed adequately, and diluted to 10 mL with ultra-pure water. After that, the fluorescence spectra were collected by an LS55 fluorophotometer under the excitation wavelength of 319 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission wavelength were both fixed at 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PA-CDs-OH− system to H+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence response of PA-CDs-OH− system to H+, 1 mL of 50 μg/mL PA-CDs, 280 μL of OH−, and 0, 20, 40, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 190, 200, 210, 220, 240, and 260 μL of H+ with concentration 0.01 mol/L were added to a series of colorimetry tubes, which were diluted to 10 mL with ultra-pure water. Then, the mixed solutions were subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. After that, the fluorescence spectra were collected with an LS55 fluorophotometer under the excitation wavelength of 319 nm, and the slit width of the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were both fixed at 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PA-CDs film for metal corrosion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The PVA/starch film was firstly cut into 7 cm by 7 cm blocks. Then, the PVA/starch film was dropped into the PA-CDs solution with the concentration of 0.2 mg/mL, maintained for about 12 h, and air-dried naturally. The as-prepared PA-CDs film was obtained for corrosion warning purposes. To investigate the response performance in corrosion monitoring, the PA-CDs film was applied to pack the Q235 carbon steel sheets, and treated with a damp-heat test at 50 °C for 0, 0.5, 1, and 2 h. After that, the fluorescence of the package films was observed under 365 nm UV light to examine its ability in corrosion monitoring.

The quenching of the fluorescence under 365 nm UV light was evaluated by total color change value (ΔE), which was obtained according to the follow equation [23]:
Δ E

In which, R0 /G0 B0 and R/G/B were the color value parameter of “red”, “green”, and “blue” colors before and after the damp-heat test, respectively. All the color value parameters were outputted by a smartphone software “Color recognition picker”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and fluorescence mechanism of PA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the effect of hydrothermal treatment temperatures and times in the PA-CDs preparation process were explored through the evaluation of the corresponding fluorescence intensity. In Fig. 1 a, the fluorescence signal indicated a gradually increasing trend from 16 a.u. to 86 a.u. at the temperature between 140 °C and 200 °C. When temperature increased from 200 °C to 260 °C, the fluorescence intensity increased from 86 a.u. to 848 a.u. Especially, the fluorescence intensity dramatically increased from 337 a.u. to 848 a.u. at the temperature between 240 °C and 260 °C. The effects of hydrothermal time on the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs were shown in Fig. 1b, and the optimal hydrothermal time was 6 h as a longer time did not contribute to increasing the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs, suggesting that the formation of PA-CDs reached the equilibrium after the 6-hour hydrothermal treatment.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to further investigate both the formation process and fluorescence mechanism of PA-CDs (Fig. 1c). Typically, D band (around 1360 cm−1) and G band (around 1580 cm−1) are the characteristic Raman bands of CDs, which corresponds to the sp3 carbon defects and sp2 graphitic carbon, respectively [25]. The PA solution itself does not show a graphic D band and G band before hydrothermal treatment, implying that PA itself does not contain the characterization structure of CDs. Under 160 °C hydrothermal treatment, obvious D band and G band can be observed (IG/ID = 1.28). Additionally, the weak bands at 1080 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 indicated the presence of CCH bending and CCAr stretching vibrations in the phenoxy group (Ph-O) [25]. These results implied that PA was decomposed into inositol fragments which were aggregated to form CDs. At 180 °C and 200 °C, the intensity ratios of IG/ID were 1.48 and 1.54, respectively, indicating the increase of sp2-domains in the PA-CDs. Besides, obvious Raman bands for C-O, Ph-O, P=O, P-O, and C-O-P were observed at 1210 cm−1, 1080 cm−1, 792 cm−1, 749 cm−1, and 576 cm−1 [26], implying the phosphate groups and other oxygen-containing groups underwent transformation into PA-CDs. At 220 °C, the intensity ratio of IG/ID was decreased to 0.90, and the blue shift of the G band from 1580 cm−1 to 1562 cm−1 was observed. These could be associated with a reduced number of the layered graphic sheets [20] due to the further decomposition of inositol into the sp3 hybrid structure. When the temperature was further increase to 240 °C and 260 °C, the G bands were still located at 1562 cm−1, implying that the number of graphic sheets layered did not change. However, the intensity ratio of IG/ID was increased to 1.14 and 1.41 respectively, indicating the increase of sp2 graphitic carbon.

Generally, the sp2 conjugated carbon can be categorized into H-aggregates and J-aggregates, corresponding to the aggregation-induced emission and aggregation-caused quenching phenomena, respectively [20]. In the case of H-aggregates, the sp2 structure undergoes vertical aggregation, leading to electron transfer from the excited state molecule to the adjacent ground state molecule, and the energy is dissipated in a non-radiative manner, rather than being releases by luminescence. For the J-aggregates, the sp2 structure undergoes aggregation in a plane, promoting the formation of a larger π-conjugation electronic system, and more electrons can transfer from the ground state to the excited state. On the other hand, physical restrictions of J-aggregates block the non-radiative relaxation pathways, promote the excitons to decay radiatively, and thus release strong fluorescence. Based on the above discussion, the formation and fluorescence mechanism of PA-CDs was summarized in Fig. 1d. Phytic acid consists of carbon skeleton and six phosphate groups. With hydrothermal treatment under 160 °C, phytic acid could be decomposed into phosphoric acid and inositol fragments, and the inositol fragments were aggregated together to form CDs. When the temperature was increased to 180 °C and 200 °C, more and more inositol fragments were stacked to form the sp2 conjugated carbon core which were surrounded by –OH, −H2PO4, and other types of oxygen-containing groups. It has been widely reported that oxygen-containing groups are an important fluorescence center for CDs [27,28]. However, fluorescence test results suggest that their contribution to fluorescence remained minimal. This may be due to the fact that the sp2 conjugated carbons were stacked in a J-aggregates form, which was classified as aggregation-caused quenching. The fluorescence produced by the fluorescence center of oxygen-containing groups was eliminated by J-aggregated sp2-carbon. Therefore, only a small fluorescence enhancement was observed.

At 220 °C, inositol was decomposed into sp3 type of carbon, leading to the disruption of H-aggregates and a certain increase in fluorescence intensity. When the temperature further increased to 240 °C and 260 °C, the steric hindrance of sp3 carbon prevented the H-aggregates, and the sp2 conjugated carbon was stacked in the J-aggregates form, resulting in significant fluorescence enhancement.

In Fig. 2 a, the as-prepared PA-CDs showed brown-yellow color in the natural light, and yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Fig. 2b displayed the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PA-CDs. The as-prepared PA-CDs showed three distinguishable excitation peaks at 225 nm, 256 nm, and 319 nm, while their fluorescence emission peaks were observed to overlap at 360 nm and 508 nm. The emission peak at 360 nm corresponds to the ultraviolet region and is imperceptible to the naked eye, therefore the yellow-green fluorescence of PA-CDs can be ascribed to the emission peak at 508 nm. The three excitation centers of 225 nm, 256 nm, and 319 nm corresponded to π ∼ π* transition of the sp2 carbon core, n-σ* transition of the isolated functional groups, and n-π* transition of the aggregated fluorescent groups, respectively [27]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of PA-CDs was determined to be 24.6 % through the utilization of quinine sulfate as a reference standard [29].

When the excitation wavelengths were increased from 200 nm to 330 nm, only the fluorescence emission intensity of PA-CDs changed, its emission position did not change obviously (from 353 nm to 362 nm) (Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d). The excitation-independent characteristics also indicate a homogeneous particle size distribution [30] of PA-CDs. A narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 46 nm was obtained for the PA-CDs, which is much narrower than that of the majority of carbon dots in previous publications (Table 1 ). According to the size effect of quantum dots, CDs of different sizes correspond to different emission wavelengths. Thus, a more uniform size distribution of CDs results in a smaller FWHM. It is evident that PA-CDs synthesized via the hydrothermal of phytic acid exhibit excellent particle size uniformity [31].

The effect of concentrations (ranging from 0.05 μg/mL to 10.00 μg/mL) on the three excitation centers of PA-CDs was investigated, and results were shown in Fig. 2e ∼ f. At low concentrations, the increase in concentrations led to an increase in all three excitation centers. The excitation center at 319 nm increased with the concentration of PA-CDs in the range of 0.05 to 7.00 μg/mL, while the increment rate decreased for concentrations higher than 7.00 μg/mL. This can be ascribed to the increase of the aggregated functional groups. However, at concentrations exceeding 7.00 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs with an excitation center at 319 nm decreased. This phenomenon can be attributed to the self-quenching effect resulting from nanoparticle collisions. As the concentration of PA-CDs was higher than 1.60 μ g/mL, the excitation centers at 256 nm were inhibited, which can be explained by the fact that more and more isolated functional groups were aggregated. Furthermore, the excitation peak at 225 nm decreased when the concentration continually increased from 1.40 to 10.00 μg/mL, because the excessive aggregated fluorescent groups prevented the excitation of the core of PA-CDs in the concentrated solution.

The prerequisite for the successful application of fluorescent probes in real environments lies in their exceptional stability. Thus, prior to the application of the fluorescent probes, the stability of PA-CDs was assessed by investigating the fluorescence intensity under varying salt concentration, storage time, and UV irradiation time. The stability of PA-CDs in NaCl solution was initially examined due to the corrosive nature typically associated with Cl−. As anticipated, the fluorescence intensities of PA-CDs remain barely affected when NaCl concentrations vary from 0 to 1.0 mol/L, thus demonstrating the exceptional salt-resistance ability of PA-CDs (Fig. 2g). The anti-photobleaching ability of PA-CDs was also proved by revealing their minuscule change of intensity after irradiation for 180 min under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2h). Advantageously, a negligible decrease in fluorescence was observed after a 30-day storage period (Fig. 2i). All these findings indicate that the as-synthesized PA-CDs possess exceptional stability, making them suitable as excellent fluorescent probes for real-time monitoring of OH− and H+ in metal corrosion.

TEM image of PA-CDs reveals that the as-prepared PA-CDs are quasi-spherical and homogeneously dispersed (Fig. 3 a). The sizes of PA-CDs were distributed in a relatively narrow range of 3.42 nm ∼ 5.09 nm, and the average particle size was estimated to be 4.23 nm. This uniformity in size and shape is further supported by the narrowed FWHM and excitation-independent properties of PA-CDs. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 3b reveals a highly micro-crystalline feature of PA-CDs, with a crystalline plane spacing of about 0.16 nm, ascribed to the (020) facet of graphitic carbon [40].

XRD analysis in Fig. 3c for PA-CDs reveals two diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 14.7° and 29.8°, which correspond to the (001) plane and (002) plane of graphite, respectively [41,42]. Specifically, the PA-CDs exhibited a distinctive diffraction peak at approximately 14.7° (d001 = 0.613), which was comparable to the standard graphite oxide's diffraction peak at 2θ = 10.6° in the (001) plane. The enhancement can be attributed to the interlayer spacing between sp3 layers [43]. During the carbonization process, functional groups are generated and subsequently attached to the edges of basal planes, thereby forming the structure of PA-CDs. This led to an increase in the intermediate spacing that exists between the layers.

The XPS survey of PA-CDs in Fig. 3d reveals three characteristic peaks at 133.1 eV, 191.6 eV, 284.3 eV, and 532.5 eV were ascribed to P 2p, P 2 s, C 1 s, and O 1 s respectively. Their atom mounts were estimated to be 2.36 %, 1.81 %, 72.68 %, and 23.25 %, respectively [19]. The carbon content of PA-CDs (72.68 %) is much higher than that of the precursor phytic acid (10.90 %), while the oxygen and phosphorus contents of PA-CDs (23.25 % and 4.17 %, respectively) are much lower than phytic acid (58.18 % and 28.18 %, respectively). These results demonstrate that with the hydrothermal treatment, phytic acid was decomposed and carbonized to form the carbon core. The ratio of O and P for −H2PO4 group is around 2, while the O/P ratio at PA-CDs is 5.58, which means that 14.91 % of the O atoms were came from other O-containing groups.

Deconvolution of the C 1 s peak can be divided into three peaks that are discernible at a binding energy of 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.6 eV in Fig. 3e, which are ascribed to C–C/C = C, C-O/C-P, and C = O, respectively [44]. The appearance of C = C further demonstrated the graphic-like structure of PA-CDs, which was in accordance with the XRD detection result, while the C–C demonstrated the disordered structure of PA-CDs. The C-O bond may correspond to epoxy and tertiary alcohol functional groups on the basal plane, as well as phenol in the periphery. The C = O indicates the presence of ketone and carboxylic groups in the graphene periphery [46]. The composition of different carbon bonds reflected the fact that most of the oxygen functional groups were located on the edge sites of the PA-CDs. P 2p in Fig. 3f is deconvoluted into two main peaks which are P-C (133.6 eV) and P-O/P = O (134.4 eV) [45,46]. The presence of the P-C peak indicates that phosphorus atoms have been incorporated into the graphene framework through a hydrothermal process [46]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s in Fig. 3g indicate three sub-peaks at 531.5 eV, 533.2 eV, and 535.7 eV which are associated with C = O/P = O, C-O/P-O, and C-OH/P-OH, respectively [45,47]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s indicated that P atoms have been doped into the carbon skeleton and the presence of abundant −H2PO4, C = O, and –OH groups in the as-prepared PA-CDs, thereby providing ample reaction sites for PA-CDs. The introduction of P atoms and the presence of abundant surface oxygen-containing groups are expected to enhance the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivity of PA-CDs [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “off–on” response of PA-CDs to OH− and H+
ce:section.ce:para:
The response of PA-CDs solution to OH−, was primarily studied as a proof-of-concept to corrosion warning and as a model reference to evaluate the detection performance. The color and fluorescence emission intensity of PA-CDs were investigated with the presence of different accounts of OH−. Fig. 4 a shows the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs at 360 nm was turned off gradually along with the incremental titration of OH− ranging from 0.00 ∼ 360 μmol/L. The curve of the fluorescence quenching intensity (F0-F) versus the concentration of OH− (cOH −) is displayed in Fig. 4b. In the concentration of 20 ∼ 160 μ mol/L, a relationship between F
0
-F of PA-CDs and cOH − can be fitted via a binomial expression of F0-F = 8.67–87.63(c
OH
− )+ 193.59(c
OH
− ) 2 (R2 = 0.9960). In the concentration range of 160 ∼ 320 μmol/L, the relationship between F0-F and cOH − can be fitted via an equation of F0-F = 2.84(c
OH
−)-29.67 (R2 = 0.9896). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 7.33 μmol/L using the equation LOD = 3 S/N [48], where S stands for the standard deviation of blank measurements by 6 times, and N is the slope of the linear fitting curve. At the same time, the gradually changed fluorescence “turn-off” can also be observed under 365 nm UV light when the OH− was added constantly (Fig. 4c). The obvious fluorescence quenching of PA-CDs induced by OH− implies the efficient fluorescence “turn-off” based method for OH− detection in the corrosion.

In order to explore the effect of H+ on the PA-CDs-OH− system, the effect of H+ on the fluorescence of PA-CDs was investigated first. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, under the concentration range of 0.00 ∼ 4.00 mmol/L, H+ showed little influence on the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs. Subsequently, 220 µmol/L of H+ were added into the PA-CDs-OH− system, and their fluorescence intensity under 0 ∼ 80 min of ultrasonic treatment was investigated, and illustrated in Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c. The original PA-CDs show a fluorescence intensity of 906 a.u. When 320 µmol/L of OH− was added, the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs decreased to 43 a.u., and more than 95 % of the fluorescence was quenched. Without ultrasonic treatment, the addition of 220 μmol/L H+ resulted in fluorescence recovery of PA-CDs less than 20 %. After ultrasonic treatment of 20 ∼ 60 min, the fluorescence of PA-CDs was recovered gradually. The highest fluorescence recovery can be obtained with the ultrasonic treatment of 60 min, and 80 % of the PA-CDs fluorescence was recovered. For the fluorescence recovery of PA-CDs-OH− system to H+, a fluorescence “turn-on” based sensor can be constructed for H+ detection.

The fluorescence change of the PA-CDs-OH− system with the addition of 0.00 ∼ 240 μmol/L H+ was discussed in detail. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b, the addition of H+ resulted in the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs-OH− system recovery (fluorescence “turn-on”) gradually in the concentrations range of 0.00 ∼ 220 μmol/L. When the concentration was larger than 220 μmol/L, the addition of H+ only resulted a slight change in the fluorescence. The inset of Fig. 6a shows that in the concentration ranges of 0 ∼ 140 μmol/L and 140 ∼ 230 μmol/L, the relationships between fluorescence intensity (F) of PA-CDs-OH− system and H+ concentration (c) can be fitted by a monomial expression and binomial expression of F = 0.39c + 56.50 (R2 = 0.9944) and F = 2531–31.38c + 0.10c2 (R2 = 0.9755), respectively. The calculated LOD was 20.34 μmol/L. The above results indicate that PA-CDs-OH− system has a potential application for the detection of the typical corrosion species H+.

As described above, the presence of OH− leads to fluorescence quenching. Interestingly, the addition of H+ resulted in only a little fluorescence recovery of PA-CDs without ultrasonic treatment, while most of the fluorescence was recovered with ultrasonic treatment of 60 min. It is known that ultrasonic treatment shows an important influence on the distribution state of nano-particles. Therefore, to get a better understanding of the fluorescence response of PA-CDs to OH−, the surface morphologies of the PA-CDs in the presence of OH− and H+ were investigated first. TEM images of PA-CDs in Fig. 7 a revealed that the introduction of OH− resulted in the aggression of PA-CDs. Without ultrasonic treatment, the introduction of H+ did not change the aggression state of PA-CDs-OH− (Fig. 7b). Under ultrasonic treatment of 60 min, the presence of H+ resulted in the re-dispersion of PA-CDs (Fig. 7c). These results supported the hypotheses that the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs was mainly affected by its distribution state.

Aggregation-induced fluorescence change of nano-particles has been widely reported [20]. Usually, the aggregation of the nano-particles resulted in the red shift of the absorption spectrum [47]. For example, in the study of Li et al. [49], in the pH range of 5 ∼ 9, the change of pH triggered the aggregation-dispersion of CDs, and finally led to the fluorescence change. At the same time, a red shift of absorption spectra can also be observed in the aggregation state. The fluorescence “off–on” mechanism of PA-CDs was further investigated by the UV–Vis spectra [50,51]. Fig. 7d indicated the red shift of the absorption band from 450 nm to 480 nm in the presence of different amounts of OH−, which further implies the aggregation of PA-CDs. The effects of H+ on absorption spectra of the PA-CDs-OH− system before and after ultrasonic treatment were also investigated. According to Fig. 7e, without ultrasonic treatment, the absorption spectrum of the PA-CDs-OH− system shows little change. After ultrasonic treatment, with the introduction of H+ the absorption spectrum of PA-CDs-OH− system was almost recovery. The absorption measurement result further demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of PA-CDs was mainly affected by its distribution state, aggregation or disaggregation.

FT-IR spectra were carried out to study the functional group change before and after the addition of OH− (Fig. 7f). FT-IR spectrum of PA-CDs shows a strong and broad absorption band from 2959 cm−1 to 3666 cm−1, which was generated from the O–H stretching. This suggests a lot of hydroxyl groups present on the surface of PA-CDs. With the hydrothermal treatment, phytic acid was decomposed into phosphoric acid and inositol fragments, and the existence of inositol led to the formation of a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon network [23]. The peaks at 2916 cm−1 and 1631 cm−1, 1415 cm−1 are due to the C–H stretching vibration in sp3 hybrid carbon, C = C stretching vibration in sp2 hybid carbon and C–C stretching vibration in sp2 hybrid carbon, which was the main component of the carbon network skeleton. The presence of a characteristic band at 2387 cm−1, 1631 cm−1,and 1448 cm−1 were determined, indicating the formation of an O = C = O, C = O, and C-O bond. These results confirmed the PA-CDs were in combination with carbon–oxygen groups, which renders them extremely soluble in water [52]. Other characteristic bands appearing at 1292 cm−1, 1097 cm−1, 919 cm−1, and 552 cm−1 are ascribed respectively to the stretching vibration of P = O from the phosphate groups, P-O, P-O-R (R = aromatic ring), and P-O–H, which indicated the attachment of phosphate-oxygen groups on the carbon skeleton. Concurrently, the stretching vibration of P-C induces a discernible peak at 698 cm−1, signifying successful phosphorus doping within the PA-CDs structure.

As it can be seen from the FT-IR spectrum of PA-CDs-OH− system, the presence of OH− led to the decrease of the absorption bands of –OH at 2959 cm−1 to 3666 cm−1 and the absorption band of −P-O–H at 552 cm−1 was weaker. These results imply the interaction between OH− and H+ of phosphate group. The addition of OH− results in the deprotonation of phosphate groups on the surface of PA-CDs and further leads to increased negative charges on PA-CDs, which can be demonstrated by the change of negative zeta potential from −3.78 mV to −24.37 mV before and after adding OH−. Meanwhile, a shoulder peak at 3040 cm−1 emerged, indicating the formation of a novel –OH species. An obvious blue shift of P = O from 1280 cm−1 to 1270 cm−1 and P-O bond from 1103 cm−1 to 989 cm−1 can be observed, suggesting the decrease in electron cloud density, which is indicative of a reduction in electronegativity. Concluding the above results, the mechanism of PA-CDs response to OH− can be described in Fig. 7g. The deprotonation effect of phosphate groups induce by OH− result in its negatively charge. When the negatively charged phosphate group encounters the hydroxyl group on the adjacent PA-CDs, it competes with the proton hydrogen on the hydroxyl group. Finally, the chelating reaction occurs and PA-CDs agglomerate, resulting in fluorescence quenching. With the addition of H+, the protonation of the surface functional phosphate groups occurs. However, the agglomerated nanoparticles require ultrasonic treatment, which is the reason for the fluorescence recovery after ultrasonic treatment.

The anti-rust film is a common carrier to be used in the anti-corrosion field [53]. Therefore, PA-CDs were fixed in the starch/PVA film (PA-CDs film) which contained a large number of hydrophilic groups and was easy to be connected with PA-CDs. In our previous study [23], the morphology, and structure of the film and PA-CDs film have been discussed in detail through the SEM, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS analysis. The morphology analysis shows that the particles on the surface of PA-CDs film were well distributed and pronounced, which could be attributed to the combination of PA-CDs with the film surface and then aggregated into larger particles. FT-IR, XRD, and XPS tests showed that PA-CDs were loaded on starch/PVA film through hydrogen bonding interaction.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, the PA-CDs film revealed obvious green-yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. With the addition of 0 ∼ 400 μmol/L OH−, the green-yellow fluorescence was turned off gradually, indicating its potential application in corrosion detection. The quenching of the fluorescence under 365 nm UV light was evaluated by ΔE. In the concentration range of 50 ∼ 400 μmol/L, the ΔE was in a good linear relationship with OH− concentration c (Fig. 8b), with linear regression equation of ΔE = 0.60c + 40.10, R2 = 0.9862. After that, the PA-CDs film was employed to pack the Q235 carbon steel (Middle of Fig. 8c), and a damp-heat test at 50 °C under 3.5 % NaCl solution was carried out to further investigate its performance in corrosion monitoring. Before the damp-heat test, the PA-CDs film showed well-proportioned fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, and the Q235 carbon steel sheet was not corroded (Fig. 8c). After 30 min of damp-heat test, a part of the fluorescence “turn-off” can be observed on the film, and the OH− concentration was calculated to be 57.94 μmol/L; while almost no corrosion can be observed by the naked eye (Fig. 8d). After 1 h and 2 h damp-heat test, the PA-CDs film showed obvious fluorescence “turn-off”, and some corrosion spitting can be observed (Fig. 8e and f). The OH− concentration was calculated to be 110 ∼ 368 μmol/L. This result demonstrates that the PA-CDs film can be applied as a portable tool in the monitoring of steel corrosion. In addition, PA-CDs can be used as a functional additive to be added to anti-rust packaging film products to realize the integration of corrosion protection and corrosion monitoring.

The engineering and economic feasibility for the production of PA-CDs and PA-CDs film is imperative for its implementation in large-scale production. The cost estimate will be contingent upon the costs of raw materials, wages for production workers, energy consumption (including water, heat, and electricity), transportation expenses, as well as other variable costs such as fluctuations in raw material prices, management expenditures, and equipment depreciation [54]. The production cost and payback of PA-CDs in China were roughly estimated, and the results are illustrated in Table 2 . The cost of producing PA-CDs amounted to 16 $/g, whereas the prevailing market price for CDs in China was significantly higher at 285.7 $/g, resulting in a payback of 269.7 $/g. The payback ratio stood at an impressive 16.85 times the total cost. It is noteworthy that the OH− sensitive PA-CDs developed in this study possess versatile applications not only in corrosion monitoring but also in rapid monitoring of food, environment, and life sciences. The cost of PA-CDs can be further reduced with the increasing market demand. Indeed, the cost of PA-CDs was estimated roughly and the market price of PA-CDs was virtuall high, but it suggested that the production of PA-CDs is economically feasible. More importantly, the preparation of PA-CDs only necessitates a simple hydrothermal treatment of phytic acid, a biomass component derived from crop seeds. The raw material for PA-CDs is green and renewable in nature. The hydrothermal treatment process solely requires electronic energy and water input, rendering it environmentally friendly.

In conclusion, the OH− and H+ sensitive PA-CDs were fabricated through hydrothermal treatment of biomass component phytic acid, and a green, fast, and simple fluorescence platform was constructed for corrosion monitoring. The following conclusions can be obtained:
(1)
When the hydrothermal temperature was below 220 °C, the sp2-conjugated carbons of PA-CDs were in H-aggregates state, and the electrons in the excited state went back to the ground state in a non-radiative transition manner rather than releasing fluorescence. When hydrothermal temperature was over 220 °C, the sp2-conjugated carbons of PA-CDs were in J-aggregates state, which promoted the excitons to decay radiatively, and thus released strong fluorescence.
(2)
PA-CDs exhibited three excitation bands (225 nm, 256 nm, or 319 nm) and dual emission bands (360 nm and 508 nm), with a FWHM of 46 nm, as well as green-yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. PA-CDs possess exceptional optical stability under varying salt concentration, storage time, and UV irradiation time.
(3)
PA-CDs were homogeneously dispersed with an average size of 4.23 nm, and reveals typical graphene-like structure. PA-CDs contained a large amount of –OH and −H2PO4, hereby providing ample reaction sites for PA-CDs.
(4)
With the presence of OH−, the fluorescence was “turn-off” due to the aggression of PA-CDs induced by the deprotonation of −H2PO4 groups, while the fluorescence was “turn-on” in the presence of H+ under 1 h ultrasonic treatment due to the re-dispersion of PA-CDs induced by the protonation of −H2PO4. The fluorescence response of PA-CDs to OH− promotes its application in metal corrosion sensing.
(5)
The as-prepared PA-CDs were loaded on ST/PVA film, and then applied to Q235 steel sheets for corrosion monitoring. Damp-heat test was performed at 50 °C and in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution demonstrated that PA-CDs film has a sensitive fluorescent “turn-off” when corrosion occurs due to the generation of OH−. This demonstrated the application of PA-CDs film to corrosion monitoring in the early stage.
(6)
The synthesis of OH−-responsive PA-CDs is economically viable, environmentally sustainable, and characterized by a clean production process.
